coredata.dc:title: A bifunctional nanozyme of carbon dots-mediated Co9S8 formation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shijia
Pang, Ernan
Li, Ning
Chang, Qing
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: Controlling the size of nanocrystals and inhibiting their agglomeration are of paramount importance for achieving ideal catalytic performance. Here we discovered that carbon dots (CDs) are not only able to serve as reductants but also as stabilizers of ultrasmall Co9S8 nanocrystals by means of their surface terminal groups. As a result, ultrasmall Co9S8 nanocrystals are incorporated into porous carbon nanosheets formed by splicing CDs. The resultant nanocomposites display a rich pore structure accompanying with large specific surface area and outstanding bifunctional performances to mimic the catalytic activity of peroxidase and oxidase without exerting any external energy. More importantly, the unique architecture endows Co9S8 nanocrystals with high stability and good durability. The nanocomposites have been demonstrated as a colorimetric sensor for detection of ascorbic acid with a superior anti-interference ability as well as a detection limit of 0.2 μM. Our findings open new synthetic opportunities by tuning the interaction of CDs with the surrounding environment and enable advanced applications such as biomedicine and catalytic transformations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanocomposite
Co9S8 nanoparticle
Controllable reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of Co9S8/C and CS-1; (b) Raman spectra of Co9S8/C and CS-1; (c and d) XPS spectra of Co 2p and S 2p of Co9S8/C；(e and f) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distributions of Co9S8/C.
TEM (a), HRTEM (b) and the elemental mapping images of Co9S8/C (c-h).

(a and b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of samples + H2O2 + TMB system and samples + TMB system at the given reaction time, the inset showing the corresponding solution photographs after reaction completion; (c) Cycle stability of Co9S8/C in POD and OXD-like activities; (d) Durability of Co9S8/C; (e) Activity comparisons of Co9S8/C before and after treatment with 1.0 M HCl; (f) Effects of the temperature on POD- and OXD-like activities of Co9S8/C as well as natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP) reported in literature.

(a) Schematic illustration of catalytic mechanism of Co9S8/C; (b) Photoluminescent spectra of TA solution in the presence of Co9S8/C + H2O2 system; (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of NBT solution in the presence of Co9S8/C; (d) CV of Co9S8/C with varied amounts of H2O2; (e) CV of Co9S8/C before and after pumping N2.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Co9S8/C + H2O2 + TMB system adding different amount of AA; (b) Photographs of Co9S8/C + H2O2 + TMB system with varied concentrations of AA; (c) Linear plots of the variations of absorbance at 652 nm (ΔA652) versus concentrations of AA; (d) Effects of various interfering substances on sensing AA for Co9S8/C + H2O2 + TMB system.
Schematic illustration of preparation of Co9S8/C.

head.ce:title: A bifunctional nanozyme of carbon dots-mediated Co9S8 formation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of New Energy Materials and Devices, North University of China, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel nanocomposite that ultrasmall Co9S8 nanocrystals are incorporated into porous carbon nanosheets formed by splicing carbon dots is achieved, and shows bifunctional enzyme-like activities without exerting any external energy.

Controlling the size of nanocrystals and inhibiting their agglomeration are of paramount importance for achieving ideal catalytic performance. Here we discovered that carbon dots (CDs) are not only able to serve as reductants but also as stabilizers of ultrasmall Co9S8 nanocrystals by means of their surface terminal groups. As a result, ultrasmall Co9S8 nanocrystals are incorporated into porous carbon nanosheets formed by splicing CDs. The resultant nanocomposites display a rich pore structure accompanying with large specific surface area and outstanding bifunctional performances to mimic the catalytic activity of peroxidase and oxidase without exerting any external energy. More importantly, the unique architecture endows Co9S8 nanocrystals with high stability and good durability. The nanocomposites have been demonstrated as a colorimetric sensor for detection of ascorbic acid with a superior anti-interference ability as well as a detection limit of 0.2 μM. Our findings open new synthetic opportunities by tuning the interaction of CDs with the surrounding environment and enable advanced applications such as biomedicine and catalytic transformations.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of zero-dimensional (0D) fluorescent nanomaterials, have aroused considerable interest on account of their excellent electronic, optical, physicochemical properties as well as low cost and a wide range of applications such as biomedicine, chemical sensing, photo- and electro-catalysis, light-emitting diodes, solar cells, lithium/sodium ion batteries [1–9]. Structurally, CDs possess carbon cores embedded in shells consisting of numerous polar groups including carboxylic acids, alcohols, and amines. These heteroatom groups on the surface of CDs determine the interaction of them with the surrounding environment in the aspect of reactivity, binding and redox properties [10–12]. By these surface groups, for instance, CDs can complex with lots of metal ions like Fe3+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ru3+, and Co2+, allowing metal precursors to be strongly adsorbed onto them [13–18]. Thus, CDs can be used as multifunctional carriers or nanosized templates to prevent severe aggregation of adducts in the preparation of nanocomposites. Besides, previous works have demonstrated that CDs can be spliced into two-dimensional (2D) carbon nanosheets under high temperature and cause the products formed on their surface to be confined [19,20]. Not only does this strategy inhibit the agglomeration of new formed nanostructures, but also promote their property stability in use. The latest work indicated that even single atoms or small compound clusters could be stabilized on the 2D carbon nanosheets formed by CDs and displayed outstanding stability and performance for the hydrogen evolution reaction over a wide pH range [17,21].

A multivalent metal ion, such as Fe2+/3+, Co2+/3+, Cu1+/2+, and Ce3+/4+, is required for the catalytic reaction to proceed. Thereby, many nanomaterials containing multivalent elements exhibit enzyme-mimicking activity and promise to become the substitutes of natural enzymes, which are commonly expensive to manufacture and store, unstable to transfer, and sensitive to harsh environments [22–27]. Transition metal Co with variable valences can form many kinds of sulfides, such as CoS, CoS2, Co2S3, Co3S4 and Co9S8, but they generally have low catalytic activity [28–34]. This mainly results from two aspects: First, it is not easy for their nanoparticles to endow with a small nanometer size and uniform distribution, limiting accessibility to the active sites. Second, the weak anchoring force with carbon substrates makes their nanoparticles suffer from instability and easy agglomeration, leading to poor durability. Recently, many efforts, e.g. the nanospace confinement method have been made to overcome the above problems [17,19,20,35,36]. However, the used preparation procedures are usually intricate, tedious, inefficient, or costly.

In this work, we developed a facile strategy for the synthesis of a novel nanocomposite that ultrasmall Co9S8 nanoparticles were incorporated into porous 2D carbon nanosheets formed by splicing CDs (named as Co9S8/C). As illustrated in Scheme 1 , the hexaamminecobalt ions were firstly adsorbed onto CDs through electrostatic interactions and then decomposed on the surface of CDs under high temperature. This process not only produced reductive gases (H2 and CO) to reduce sulfamate ions in the surface layer and leaded to Co9S8 formation upon CDs, but also created abundant pores in 2D nanosheets with CDs’ splicing reactions. As a result, as-prepared Co9S8/C showed a rich pore structure accompanying with large specific surface area and outstanding bifunctional performances to mimic the catalytic activity of peroxidase (POD) and oxidase (OXD) without exerting any external energy. Based on this outcome, Co9S8/C has been demonstrated as a colorimetric sensor for detection of ascorbic acid (AA) as well and exhibited a superior anti-interference ability as well as a detection limit of 0.2 μM.

The CDs were prepared by the selective oxidization of coal pitch powder with a mixed solution of formic acid and H2O2 [37,38]. After purification via dialysis and drying at 60 °C, the resultant powders of CDs (100 mg) were further dispersed in 15 mL ethanol solvent to obtain Solution A. Meanwhile, 100 mg of Co(SO3NH2)2·xH2O was dissolved in 15 mL ammonia solution to obtain Solution B. Afterward, Solution A was added dropwise to Solution B under vigorous stirring conditions and then dried at 70 °C to obtain solid powders. As illustrated in Scheme 1, these powders were annealed at 600 °C for 2 h in a tube furnace under an Ar atmosphere (heating ramp 5 °C min−1). Last, the black powders of Co9S8/C were collected when the temperature dropped to room temperature. The contrast samples (CS-1, CS-2, CS-3) were prepared using a procedure similar to that described above for Co9S8/C, except for the different CDs with suitable amount. The CS-4 was prepared by directly calcining CDs at 600 °C for 2 h. The other samples of CS-5 and CS-6 were obtained via the same procedure with Co9S8/C, except for the different calcination temperatures (400 °C, 800 °C). The synthesis parameters of all samples were listed in Table S1. Further sample characterizations and experimental details were provided in Supplementary data.

Fig. 1 a shows X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of two samples. The sample (Co9S8/C) obtained in the presence of CDs displays the diffraction peaks corresponding well to standard XRD patterns of Co9S8 (PDF#75–2023), whereas the contrast sample (CS-1) without adding CDs merely presents the diffraction peaks of CoSO4 phase (PDF#82–0185). This verifies the vital roles of CDs in the formation of Co9S8 during pyrolysis process. Raman spectroscopy was conducted to uncover the evolution structure of CDs (Fig. 1b). For the sample of Co9S8/C, the Raman peaks at 663.5 and 460.4 cm−1 belonging to the Raman bands of Co9S8 [39] and those at 1340 and 1580 cm−1 assigning to carbon D and G bands are observed clearly, respectively. For CS-1, there is no any peak in its Raman spectra, suggesting absence of carbon structure and Co9S8 phase. Accordingly, Raman spectra confirm the coexistence of carbon nanostructures and Co9S8 in the sample of Co9S8/C. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to clarify chemical composition and element valence states of the Co9S8/C. Its full survey-scan spectrum reveals the presence of Co (3.79 at.%), S (4.03 at.%), C (68.71 at.%), O (19.1 at.%) and N (4.36 at.%) elements (Figure S1), indicating that the weight percentage of Co9S8 is around 21.47% in the composite. By analysis of XPS Co 2p spectrum, the peaks at 778.5 and 793.3 eV are ascribed to Co 2P 3/2 and Co 2p 1/2 of Co3+, while 781.1 and 796.8 eV to Co 2P 3/2 and Co 2p 1/2 of Co2+, respectively (Fig. 1c) [40,41]. The corresponding XPS S 2p spectrum presents the peaks at 161.3 and 162.4 eV that are indexed to Co-S bonds in Co9S8/C, the peaks at 163.5 and 164.1 eV that are assigned to CSC bonds, and the peaks at 168.5 eV and 169.5 eV correspond to SOx
n
− species derived from unreduced sulfur oxide on the surface of CDs (Fig. 1d) [40,41]. The CSC bond formation suggests that the CDs involve in the reduction of sulfur oxide at gas–solid interface, leading to the reactions of cobalt and sulfur on their surface. As thus, the formed Co9S8 is able to be strongly anchored onto CDs.

N2 sorption measurements reveal that the Co9S8/C sample is a porous structure with a high Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of about 233.44 m2 g−1, which is larger 38 times than the CS-1 (Fig. 1e). The pore size distribution calculated by the Barrett-Joyner-Helenda (BJH) model is mainly in the range of several nanometers (Fig. 1f). Since huge amounts of gas could be produced with the decomposition of ammonium compounds, gas escaping may cause the small mesoporous formation in the samples. The less pores and smaller specific surface area are merely achieved without the contribution of CDs, implying that the decomposition of surface groups on CDs boosts the generation of more pores. To certify it, we tested N2 sorption isotherms of the other contrast samples (CS-2 and CS-3, their XRD patterns showing in Figure S2) obtained by changing the adding amount of CDs. It can be clearly found that the BET surface area of Co9S8/C increases with the rise of CDs’ amount (Figure S3). Moreover, the annealing temperature generally determines the decomposition of ammonium compounds and surface groups on CDs as well as reaction activities of precursors. As expected, the CS-5 obtained at 400 °C has a small BET area of 23.17 m2g−1 and contains a mass of intermediates (CuSO4), while the CS-6 obtained at 800 °C has a large BET area of 249.4 m2g−1 and presents a small quantity of elemental Co (Figure S4a and b).

The morphology of Co9S8/C was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From Fig. 2 a, numerous Co9S8 nanoparticles are evenly distributed on the large carbon nanosheets. The corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows that the nanoparticle diameters ranges from 2 to10 nm, and are especially small at the border of carbon nanosheets. A typical lattice fringe of 0.29 nm, which corresponds to the interplanar spacing of the (311) plane of Co9S8, can be apparently observed in nanocrystals (Fig. 2b). Furthermore, the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping reveals that Co, S, O, N, C are homogeneously distributed over the entire sample of Co9S8/C (Fig. 2c-h), indicating good dispersity of ultrasmall Co9S8 nanoparticles upon carbon nanosheets.

The POD-like activity and OXD-like activity of Co9S8/C were evaluated using TMB as a chromogenic substrate. In the presence of H2O2, Co9S8/C can efficiently catalyze the oxidation of TMB to produce a blue color reaction, with characteristic absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. 3 a). In contrast, a significantly weaker absorption peak at 652 nm is observed from the samples of CS-1 and CS-4, indicating that the POD-like activity of Co9S8/C is much higher than them. More interestingly, Co9S8/C is also capable of catalyzing the oxidation of TMB in the absence of H2O2 analogous to OXD-like activity, whereas both CS-1 and CS-4 have almost no OXD-like activity to TMB under the same conditions (Fig. 3b). To clarify the effect of annealing temperatures on catalytic performance of samples, the enzyme-like activities of CS-5 and CS-6 were further examined under the same conditions. From Figure S5, the CS-5 possesses much lower POD- and OXD-like activities than Co9S8/C. Although the CS-6 exhibits a similar OXD-like activity with Co9S8/C, its POD-like activity is still inferior to that of Co9S8/C. Additionally, the CS-2 holds a larger BET surface area than Co9S8/C, but it still suffers from lower activity on account of less content of Co9S8 (Figure S6). As thus, POD- and OXD-like activities of as-prepared Co9S8/C stem from Co9S8 nanoparticles.

Furthermore, pH-dependent investigations of the Co9S8/C–catalyzed oxidation of TMB reveal that the activity of Co9S8/C decreases as the pH value of the buffered solution rises from pH 4.0 to 7.0. As shown in Figure S7, the enzyme-like catalytic performance of Co9S8/C performs optimally at pH 4.0. Meanwhile, the effects of the concentrations of catalyst and TMB on enzyme-like activities were investigated. From Figure S8, POD- and OXD-like activities of Co9S8/C almost linearly enhance with its concentrations when the concentrations of TMB (1.0 mM) and H2O2 (40 mM) keep unchanged. Under a given concentration of Co9S8/C, however, its ability to oxidize TMBin the buffered solution of pH 4.0 firstlyincrease andthenslightly decreasewith theincreasingofthe concentration ofTMB. The steady-state kinetic experiments were conducted to acquire enzyme kinetic parameters. The Michaelis-Menten curves for POD-like activity of Co9S8/C were provided in Figure S9a and b. By fitting, the obtained the Michaelis constant (Km) and the maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) of Co9S8/C with TMB as the substrate were 0.64 mM and 7.91 × 10−8 M s−1, and the Km and Vmax of Co9S8/C with H2O2 as the substrate were 10.23 mM and 6.05 × 10−8 M s−1, respectively. Likewise, Figure S9c shows the Michaelis-Menten curve for OXD-like activity. Based on it, the corresponding Km and Vmax values of Co9S8/C with TMB as the substrate were achieved as 0.123 mM and 2.38 × 10−8 M s−1.

As shown in Fig. 3c, Co9S8 nanoparticles upon CDs-spliced nanosheets suffers a tiny loss in activity during the cycles, which may be brought by a reason that a small quantity of samples cannot be recovered during the cycles through centrifugation. Moreover, they exhibit long-term stability and merely a little decay after at least 90 days in an open-air environment (Fig. 3d). To further confirm it, the sample of Co9S8/C was treated by soaking in 1 M HCl for 24 h. and it still kept high POD- and OXD-like activities (Fig. 3e), suggesting the important roles of CDs played in protecting Co9S8 nanoparticles. More interestingly, Co9S8 nanoparticles manifest the highest activity at the temperature of 50–60 °C, in which most of the natural peroxidase did not have any activity [42] (Fig. 3f).

Mechanistically, the bifunctional enzyme-like activities of Co9S8/C could be attributed to Co2+/Co3+ flip-flops that initiate catalytic reactions illustrated in Fig. 4 a. For POD-like reaction, H2O2 is first adsorbed to the Co9S8/C surface and the OO bonds break down to generate
HO
·
radicals by reacting with Co2+ as follows,
(1)
Co
2 +
+
H 2
O 2
→
Co
3 +
+
OH
-
+
HO
·

Based on the reaction of
HO
·
radicals with terephthalic acid (TA) to generate 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid (TAOH) with fluorescence emission at around 426 nm [27], we identified the existence of
HO
·
radicals during the enzyme reaction using Co9S8/C in the presence of H2O2 (Fig. 4b). In the meantime, H2O2 can reduce Co3+ to Co2+ by reaction (2) on the basis of Fenton reaction mechanism.
(2)
H 2
O 2
+
Co
3 +
→
Co
2 +
+
2 H
+
+
O 2
Nevertheless, the rate of reaction (2) is usually slower than that of reaction (1).

For OXD-like reaction, the role of O2 is crucial. To demonstrate it, we pumped N2 and O2 in reaction system and found that such operations remarkably influenced the catalytic activity of Co9S8/C (Figure S10a and b). Oxygen as the electron acceptor can oxidize Co2+ to Co3+ like as
(3)
Co
2 +
+
O 2
→
Co
3 +
+
O
2
· -
(4)
O
2
· -
+ 2
H
+
+ e →
H 2
O 2

By the reaction of
O
2
· -
with the nitro blue tetrazorium (NBT), as provided in Fig. 4c, the NBT characteristic absorption peak at 250 nm significantly decreases after adding Co9S8/C, revealing the generation of abundant
O
2
· -
radicals as only Co9S8/C is present.

To further examine Co2+/Co3+ redox process, we performed the cyclic voltammogram (CV) measurements. Fig. 4d illustrates the CV curves of the Co9S8/C modified glassy carbon electrode (Co9S8/C@GCE) in 1 M NaOH aqueous solution containing various H2O2 concentrations of 0 mM, 0.1 mM and 0.5 mM, respectively, at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in ambient atmosphere. An obvious hysteresis loop appears for Co9S8/C@GCE. In contrast, the same case does not occur in the bare electrode. Meanwhile, the anodic currents are raised accompanying by the reduction of the cathodic peak currents when the concentration of H2O2 increases. The above results certify that Co9S8/C can promote H2O2 decomposition to boost the oxidation of Co2+ under electric field, which is analogous to electrocatalytic behaviors. In the absence of H2O2, we compared CV curves of Co9S8/C@GCE in different atmosphere. From Fig. 4e, Co2+/Co3+ redox process is markedly retarded by pumping N2 into reaction solution, suggesting oxygen serves as an electron mediator in the redox process.

AA is an essential substance for the human body and has been widely used for the prevention and treatment of the cardiovascular disease, cancer, common cold and others [43]. Accordingly, the identification of AA content in tablets is of paramount task for pharmaceuticals [44–46]. Phenol hydroxyl groups endow AA with excellent antioxidant property. As AA is present, the oxidation product of TMB (TMBox) will be restored to TMB through two-electron reduction, leading to the blue color fading [43]. Thus, the detection of AA can be realized by monitoring the color fading. The mixed system of Co9S8/C, H2O2 and TMB was first prepared in accordance with optimalproportion and employed to detect the content of AA. Fig. 5 a displays that the absorbance of as-prepared system is gradually decreased with the increase of AA concentrations. It can be also observed directly that the blue color fades as the AA concentrations rise (Fig. 5b). The resultant linear relationship between the absorbance change (ΔA652 nm) and the AA concentration follows the equation ΔA652 nm = 0.0187[AA] + 0.0667 from 0.5 to 20 μM (R2 = 0.991). The limit detection reaches 0.2 μM, (Fig. 5c) which is better than other nanomaterial-based colorimetric detection of AA (Table S2). This sensing system was further used to identify the content of AA in Vitamin C tablet from pharmaceutical factory (Figure S11). The content of AA in Vitamin C tablet detected using this method is 59.64 mg, which is consistent with the value (60 mg) labeled by pharmaceutical factory. The influences of coexisting substances on the detection of AA were also explored to determine the selectivity of our presented system. Apart from dopamine (DA), from Fig. 5d, no any apparent changes in absorbance are observed with the addition of other interferes even if their concentrations are enhanced as beyond 10-fold that of AA, suggesting a good specificity of our presented system to AA. Noticeably, DA can cause TMB oxidation further and results in an obvious absorbance change, but its reaction mechanism is totally opposite to that of AA. Thereby, the presence of DA is able to be first identified from the detection system.

We found the role of the surface terminal groups of CDs played in governing synthetic reactions and nanocrystal formation. Not only did CDs trigger reduction reaction, but also stabilized ultrasmall nanocrystals formed upon them to inhibit their growth and agglomeration. As a result, Co9S8 nanocrystals were anchored on porous carbon nanosheets yielded by splicing CDs. The resultant Co9S8/C possesses abundant mesoporous, large specific surface area, high stability and good durability. It also exhibits outstanding bifunctional performances to mimic the catalytic activity of peroxidase and oxidase and can be used as a colorimetric sensor for detection of ascorbic acid with superior anti-interference abilities. Our findings pave a new synthetic pathway to design advanced nanocatalysts by tuning the interaction of CDs with the surrounding environment.

Shijia Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ernan Pang: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Ning Li: Validation, Visualization, Formal analysis. Qing Chang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Jinlong Yang: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Shengliang Hu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province, Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi, Key Research and Development Plan (International Cooperation) of Shanxi Province (201903D421082), Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.10.103.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
